million in today's money. Oh, but whatever way you choose to look at it it's been a shitty month for Hollywood movies, and it's been driven mostly by a complete lack of anything worth watching. it could never hope to compete with the big wannabe blockbusters. So instead, it got punted to the October dumping grounds. one movie that could have actually turned it around was Frankenstein. theatrical momentum. Basically, people just aren't that motivated to go to the movies right now. And the longer this goes on, the less likely they are to come back. And that particular problem can be traced all the way back to the so-called summer blockbuster season which consisted mostly of disappointing sequels, awful remakes, and dreary franchise movies that we've seen a million times before. The superhero genre continued its sad descent into cultural irrelevance. All three of the

big Marvel movies failed to make an impact. And even James Gunn's Superman by James Gun barely managed to struggle past 600 million. I don't care what anyone tries to tell you. Instead of being pumped up and inspired by a packed season of hit films that became cultural events, the mood heading into autumn was more like quiet disappointment. Like, is

that it? Is that all we're getting this year? It wasn't exactly helped by a dismal August and September populated almost entirely by re-releases and stale holdovers from the summer. decent big budget summer movies to come out was Joseph Kazinsk's F1, a film that actually left me feeling good in a way I hadn't since Top Gun Maverick. And most importantly another movie to add to the list of films that nobody really talked about after it left theaters. The point I'm making with all this long- winded [ __ ] is that Hollywood failed to

generate the kind of cultural and financial momentum over the summer that it really needs to keep things ticking over until the holiday season. It's been a depressingly uninspiring year for the most part with nothing to really get fired up and passionate about and it's leaving people with fewer and fewer reasons to go to the movies. Then there's the other problems that have been brewing for years now and are really starting to bite in 2025, like the rise of streaming services and the

increasingly short release windows that are actively incentivizing people not to bother with the cinema experience.
